worth_it_explanation: User permissions are a security and accountability control. When every seat member holds admin access regardless of what they actually do, the account has more ways to be changed than the work requires, and departures or agency transitions that go unactioned leave the seat reachable by people who no longer work on it. The check is a quick read of the seat's user list and role assignments. The fix is to adjust or remove access, not to rebuild anything, and it closes a gap that is straightforward to address once the user list is reviewed.
intro: The Trade Desk organizes access at the seat level. Each user is assigned a role that determines what they can change: an admin can manage users and seat settings, a manager can edit campaigns and ad groups, and a reporter can view data without making changes. Access that is wider than the work needs, access that outlives the working relationship, or agency access scoped to the full seat rather than the advertisers they manage all represent risk to the account. These checks confirm that each user holds the lowest role their work requires, that no departed person or former partner still has a path in, and that agency or partner access is limited to the advertisers they are actively managing.

audit_checks.title: Users hold broader roles than their work requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Admin access in The Trade Desk seat can manage users, edit seat-level settings, and reach every advertiser under the seat. Manager access can change campaigns, bids, and budgets. When people who only review performance hold those roles, the account carries more ways to be altered than the work justifies, including the risk of an unintended bid or budget change reaching live campaigns.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk assigns each seat user a role that sets the boundaries of what they can do. Admin users can manage other users, adjust seat-level settings, and reach every advertiser in the seat. Manager users can create and edit campaigns, ad groups, and creatives, but cannot manage other users. Reporter users can view data and pull reports without making any changes.

Least-privilege access means granting each person the lowest role their work actually requires. When a stakeholder who only reviews performance data holds an admin or manager role, the account has more exposure than the work justifies: that person can change spend, and an admin can add or remove other users entirely. The risk is not limited to bad intent. A well-meaning person can make a change in the wrong advertiser or click the wrong control while navigating the platform.

The seat's user list shows every assigned user and their role. A few signs point to access that is too broad:
  • Stakeholders who only consume reporting hold manager or admin roles rather than reporter roles
  • More than one or two people hold admin access without a clear need to manage users or seat settings
  • Role assignments do not reflect what each person actually does in the platform day to day
You may want to confirm with the client what each user's actual job in the account is before adjusting any role, so a reduction does not block real work.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce account, finance or merchandising stakeholders may want visibility into return on ad spend without needing to touch campaigns. You may want to move those viewers to reporter access so they keep the data they rely on without the ability to change spend.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, sales teams often review cost-per-lead performance without editing anything in the platform. Reporter access gives them that visibility without the ability to change campaign settings or budgets.

investigate.intro: Review the seat's user list and confirm each person holds the lowest role their work requires.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Navigate to the seat-level settings and open the user management section. List every user with their assigned role.
For each user, note what they actually do in the account, for example edit campaigns, manage other users, or only view reporting.
Flag any user whose role is higher than their work requires, for example a reporting viewer with manager or admin access.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per user, their assigned role and the work their role actually requires. A user whose role matches the lowest level their work needs passes. A user holding a role wider than their work requires is a fail recorded with the user's name and the role that would fit their actual responsibilities.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's role and the work their responsibilities require above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} users on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk seat who hold broader access than their work requires, such as stakeholders who only review reporting but hold manager or admin roles. The wider access lets more people change campaigns, budgets, or seat settings than the work justifies, which raises the chance of an accidental or unauthorized change reaching live spend. {{voice_recommend}} confirming with the client what each person actually does in the platform and then lowering any user to the least role their work needs, so the account is protected without blocking real access.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff or former partners still hold seat access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Access that outlives the working relationship leaves a live seat reachable by people who no longer work on it. A former employee or an agency whose engagement has ended can still view campaign performance and spend data, and depending on their role, can still make changes. Because the access goes unused day to day, no one is actively watching it.

educate.base:
Access to a The Trade Desk seat persists until someone explicitly removes it. When an employee leaves, an agency engagement closes, or a contractor finishes a project, their user account stays in place unless a seat admin revokes it. The seat then carries users who no longer have a reason to be there.

Stale access is a security gap independent of intent. A former agency can still see campaign performance, spend, and audience data. A former employee with manager or admin access can still alter campaigns or, in the admin case, manage other users. Because the access is not being actively used, it is not being actively monitored either, so a problem can go unnoticed.

The seat user list shows every account with access and the email domain each belongs to. A few signs point to access that should be revoked:
  • Email addresses in the user list belong to a former agency or a person who has left the business
  • Users appear in the list that no one on the current team recognizes
  • A user was added for a defined project or campaign that has since ended
You may want to confirm each unfamiliar user with the client before removing them, so an active partner is not cut off by mistake.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce account, a former agency with lingering access can still see revenue-tied performance data such as conversion values and return on ad spend. You may want to revoke any past agency's access promptly when the engagement ends so commercial performance data is not visible to a party no longer under contract.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a former agency or media vendor with remaining access can still view lead volume and cost-per-lead data. Revoking access when the relationship ends limits exposure of that pipeline data to current partners only.

investigate.intro: Review the seat's user list for anyone outside the current working relationship and confirm whether their access should be removed.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Navigate to seat-level settings and open the user management section. List every user and the email domain their account belongs to.
Compare the list against the current internal team and any active agency, contractor, or technology partner.
Flag any user whose domain belongs to a former agency, a person who has left the business, or anyone the current team cannot identify.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per user, whether they are part of the current working relationship. A user who is active and current passes. A user from a former agency, a departed employee, or an unrecognized account is a fail recorded with that user's name and email, pending the client's confirmation before removal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user against the current team and active partners above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} users on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk seat who are outside the current working relationship, such as a former agency or a departed employee whose access was never revoked. That standing access lets people who no longer work on the account view campaign performance and spend data, and depending on their role, make changes to it. Because the access goes unused day to day, no one is watching it. {{voice_recommend}} confirming each unfamiliar user with the client and then removing access for anyone outside the current relationship, so only the people actively working on the account can reach it.

audit_checks.title: Agency or partner access is scoped to the full seat rather than the advertisers they manage
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When an agency or partner is granted access at the seat level, they can see every advertiser under the seat, including advertisers they do not manage. On a seat that serves multiple brands, that means one agency can view another brand's campaign data, spend, and audience setup. Scoping their access to only the advertisers in their remit removes that exposure without changing anything about how they work.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk allows access to be scoped to specific advertisers within a seat rather than the full seat. When an agency or media partner is added with seat-level access, they can navigate to any advertiser under that seat, including brands they have no remit to manage.

On a seat that holds a single brand, this distinction has limited consequence. On a seat that serves several brands, a direct-response agency managing one brand can see the campaign structure, budgets, creative strategy, and audience setup of an entirely separate brand. That visibility may expose competitively sensitive information and violates a basic expectation most clients hold that their data is visible only to the people managing it.

The Trade Desk supports advertiser-level access grants that limit a user's view to the specific advertisers assigned to them. Granting agencies at this narrower level rather than at the seat level is the safer default. A few signs the current setup may be too broad:
  • An agency or partner user can navigate to advertisers they do not manage and see their campaign data
  • Access was granted at the seat level for convenience rather than because the partner needs full seat visibility
  • More than one agency holds seat-level access on a seat with multiple separate brand advertisers
You may want to confirm with the client what each partner actually manages before adjusting their scope, so the change reflects the real working relationship rather than a guess.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce seat running multiple brand advertiser accounts, an agency handling one brand can, with seat-level access, view the conversion values, return on ad spend, and product-level audience data of another brand. Scoping each agency to their own advertisers prevents that cross-brand data exposure.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a seat running lead-generation programs for separate clients, one agency with seat-level access can see the lead volume, cost-per-lead, and audience targeting of a competing client's program. Advertiser-scoped access keeps each agency limited to their own program's data.

investigate.intro: Review whether agency or partner users have seat-level access on a seat that holds multiple separate brand advertisers, and confirm whether their access should be narrowed to the advertisers they actually manage.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Navigate to seat-level settings and open the user management section. For each agency or partner user, note whether their access is set at the seat level or scoped to specific advertisers.
Confirm how many separate brand advertisers the seat holds. If the seat serves only one brand, seat-level access for an agency carries lower cross-brand risk.
For any agency user with seat-level access on a multi-brand seat, note the advertisers they actually manage and flag whether their current access lets them see advertisers outside that scope.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per agency or partner user, whether their access is scoped to the seat or to specific advertisers, and how many separate brand advertisers the seat holds. A seat with a single brand advertiser or a partner whose access is already scoped to only their advertisers passes. A partner with seat-level access on a multi-brand seat who can reach advertisers outside their remit is a fail recorded with the partner name and the advertisers they should be limited to.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each agency or partner user's access scope and the advertisers they actually manage above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more agency or partner users on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk seat hold access scoped to the full seat rather than the advertisers they manage. On a seat with multiple separate brand advertisers, that scope lets a partner managing one brand view the campaign data, budgets, and audience setup of another. {{voice_recommend}} narrowing each agency's access to the specific advertisers in their remit, confirming the scope with the client before making changes, so each partner can see only the work that belongs to them.
